item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic as always Rutherford. A creative concept explained well with great execution and character.

item.title: Concepts are Key
item.content:encoded: Great concept and amazing creativity with a real-life theater. This goes to show if you fictionize something it can really be amazing. Again, great concept, it's a +1 from me.

item.title: Re: Where's the +2 button
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the read! I'm really glad you liked the tone, pacing, and humor and character work :)

As for your nitpick, I can kinda' see what you're getting at. Writers should always endeavor to make the core article stand on it's own merits, but I have difficulty writing that way. To me, the conprocs and desc. should be indicative of a moment in time - this article was written during initial containment, following the exploration logs as they occur in "real time" relative to the article. It ends around the time Lyydia and Yasuo are being debriefed, and the file is pending an update.

Anyway, thanks for the in-depth review!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much for the read! I'm glad you enjoyed it :)

item.title: Where's the +2 button
item.content:encoded:
A fantastic read. I'm most impressed by how you kept just the right amount of humor through out the piece. It allowed characters be remain relatable but didn't lose the mystery or spookiness (is that the right word?) of the whole situation. The explanation of Lock Scenarios was also a very nice touch to keep the clinical tone of the entry.

spoilers and one nitpick
-It's more of a challenge than a nitpick

I got a little nervous when I saw the article kept going after what I felt was already a great ending with exploration log 4. Then you go and show off your ability to combine believable characters with realistic and conflicting motivations with the after action report. Just a fantastic tale overall.

So, after getting all these high expectations for your writing, I went and read the SCP entry proper and realized my only nitpick with the whole thing: you snuck in a fantastic SCP Tale into a not quite finished SCP. I feel that if (for whatever weird reason) someone doesn't read the Exploration Log, then the entry feels a lot less powerful. This nitpick isn't nearly big enough to kill my enjoyment of the article as a whole, but it did give me a thought: since you've exposed yourself as a skilled writer, I'm betting you could make the SCP entry stronger on it's own. In particular, I feel the "Reclassification to Neutralized pending," from the 4th log is an idea that has plenty of room to play with in the SCP entry itself…

There's actually a lot of room with a lot of different concepts in the logs… like, a lot a lot…

Damn it, where's the +2 vote button?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoiler
- Hide

I just love this! The Concept of Lockdown, the narrative, all of it!
But at most I love, that you show this more vulnerable site of realitybenders.
That having this kind of power as a kid, doesn't make anything immediately fine and perfect in your live.
The Conversation of the MTF feels real, authentic and gives great insight in their characters and left me wanting to read more about them.
So absolute a +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey! I'm glad you enjoyed the new terminology, and the story built from it. Thanks for the read!

Also, absolutely. After all, the concept of the Kant Counter, Humes, and Reality Benders are all concepts brought over from other articles - I'd be overjoyed to see Locked Scenarios become an SCP staple too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much! I'm glad you enjoyed it :) I always try to add a human angle to these kind of articles, and I'm glad it landed for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt a lot of things. I really like the character building for Yasuo, for the reality-warper kid, the sheer empathy in our agent boy's motives, heck, even the way the scenario breaks down.

I'd like to think that the Locked Scenario definition was the hook for me, and the footnotes reeled me in even further.

+1, and up there in my favorites.

Would it be possible for me to write a Locked Scenario skip at some point?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was, genuinely, a fantastic read. I love seeing more, well, human stories, and this one was engaging and very elegantly-written. You hit a really good balance between a fun and interesting concept and some strong emotional moments, and I feel like they both carry your article in equal measure. Bonus points for theater, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll take it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 Because I saw "The Mask" at that theater.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: amazing one, great story with a satisfying ending, I absolutely love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks so much for the review ShamrockJolnes! That particular "86 bodies dropping" line was actually a late addition to the article. I wanted to show a kind of full-span breakdown of the Locked Scenario once Higashi begins speaking to the kid. First it spills into the performance itself, then into the audience, then the entire scenario becomes one big stage play about what the kid is going through.

I've been working on trying to write more short form Woedenaz style articles, but it's very tricky for me. I'm glad my articles have been strong enough to keep critical, thoughtful readers like you invested until the end :) Cheers!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this one! Balancing a small-scale, human story with a larger, anomalous event isn't always easy, but this SCP feels like it strikes that balance perfectly. Codifying "locked scenarios" is a great bit of worldbuilding, and also helps draw out the mystery of why this particular locked scenario is occurring, to great effect. The moment when the pieces finally fall into place, with that line about "the sound of 86 bodies hitting the floor," was a really neat, elegant piece of storytelling in particular.

I tend not to be a big fan of skips with lengthy exploration logs/action reports/etc., but this one was a major exception for how well characterized the members of Xi-5 were. You did a lot to give them a ton of personality in a short space, and the resolution of the story wouldn't have worked nearly as well without it..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback Squamous. Writing purely clinical SCPs has never been my strength, as my draft graveyard can attest. Still, I'm glad my more "cinematic" format landed for you, and you enjoyed the concepts put forth with this article. Thanks for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A lot of the time I'm not really fond of these, how would you call them, "cinematic" SCPs? Where there's a lot of time devoted to what's effectively a script of an MTF or agents or D-class talking, with a lot of emotional value and characterisation - I feel like it's a bit too much of a break from the site's generally clinical tone.
Ultimately though, SCP files are stories, and sometimes it's best to tell a story with some actual characters, acting human, bantering and doing what they think is right. This is a time where it definitely lands, so +1.

The Locked Scenario explanation is also really good, and I hope more people pick up on it and use it in their canons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the read! Your "wow" comment genuinely made my day :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done! Really enjoyable. Echoing previous comments, I liked the clear explanation for locked scenarios. The interplay between the MTF agents was also good, just enough to give us some idea of character without being over-done.

But what really got me was finishing the article, then going to check the title.

That got a whispered, "Wow…" from me.

Excellent!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love every bit of this and the ending was just amazing +1

item.title: Re: Close to home
item.content:encoded: That's always fun, yeah :) I remember how happy I got when I saw SCPs based in Toronto.

item.title: Close to home
item.content:encoded: I live in PA, and I’ve been to Ambler theater, several times and it’s nice to have an SCP about a place I’ve been to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cheers! I'm all for expanding the set definitions within the Foundation, so long as they're well defined and easily understood at a glance. Over-saturating articles with in-universe terminology tends to discourage new readers though, aka "The heck is a hume" syndrome", so I'm trying to be conservative with them.

Also, some of my favorite articles on the site are almost entirely logs! There's room for all varieties of article here, so long as the format serves the narrative. Thanks for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Firstly, I was about ready to upvote already at the definition of Locked Scenario, I know it's not exactly traditional "good writing" but I always am a sucker for more bits of terminology being added to the Foundation lexicon.

The actual meat of the article though, is fantastic. I know people have issues with the "most of the article is in the logs" thing but it works really well here, and in general I love what you've continued to do with Newton's Bullies / Xi-5 and I hope we continue to see them in the future.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice catch! Fixed. And thanks for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Should be "heard" right?
"A sniffling sound is hear near 5-2."

Anyways, great entry. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much Alistair!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one! It's an amazing premise carried out to an amazing finish and entertaining throughout. As soon as I finished I needed to give you the +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for spotting those errors! I always seem to mix up appraised and apprised. Also, no kidding about the scenes vs acts! The last time I saw it on stage they used acts, but they also condensed it down to 10 "acts". I'll revise the article to reflect this soon. Thanks so much for the insight, and the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read SCP-4382 a few months ago; enjoyed this one no less. I really like how this article both expounds the concept of Locked Scenarios and provides a no-frills glimpse into domestic dysfunction. :)

+ Couple of errors I noticed
- Couple of errors I noticed

What? Oh, no, no this guy just sitting in the seat next to the subject. Sa- 5-1 signaled me to take him out, so I followed him into bathroom. I tried to play nice, really, but this guy really, really wants to get back to his seat!

Should it be "just this guy" and "into the bathroom" respectively?

(…) Copy, 5-4. Keep us appraised.

apprised?

Another minor gripe, A Streetcar Named Desire in its original production comprised twelve scenes, and did not utilise act divisions. No biggie though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank so much! I like to imagine there's a lot of agent cross-over with the SCP and GOC, given the similar skillset and motivations required for the job. Some people, as they grow as people, simply decide that either some measures go too far, or don't go far enough. Glad you enjoyed it :)

item.title: Re: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded: Thanks as always gee! I've corrected the two spelling mistakes, and switched things over to military time. Glad you enjoyed this :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really, really great. I love the concept of Locked Scenarios and especially love how this piece beautifully pairs with SCP-4231 and Dr. Clef's origin with the GOC's treatment of reality benders. Where the Foundation draws the line between the greater good and excessive cruelty is a really interesting theme. Also, the extended Streetcar was really funny.

Big +1

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
[+ Click to access feedback]
– hide block

along with any number of core scientific principals.

Principles?

For more information on Locked Scenarios, see Except-LS below.

Excerpt-LS

Oh yes, of course I miss my husband. He's had to skip our daughter's birthday twice now! Still, that play must be spectacular." -Marsha Kieth, 2020.

I'm unsure this footnote is necessary at all. The sentence in the document gets the meaning across.

SCP-5858-1 through 13 14 are the staff of the Ambler Theater, uniquely affected by the Locked Scenario.

I don't think the struckthrough 13 is really enough of a benefit here, since the development is more authentic in the table, especially with how close the two are to each other. This may be more of a personal point, but I feel the second clause here would benefit from a 'who are' or something similar.

Goodevening. Doyouhaveyourtickets?

I don't feel as if the Foundation would transcribe audio without spaces. Simply mentioning that they speak quickly should be sufficient and easier to read.

Date: 15/02/2020 | 11:14 AM

I like to imagine that a scientific/military organisation would use 24-hour time.

I liked this a lot. The excerpt of LS scenarios was short enough to keep me interested, while not being too short to convey any useful information. It's a cool thing to establish in the article, and it definitely worked for me. Dialogue and characterisation were realistic, and the narrative kept me hooked most of the way through. The combined length of logs 1 and 2 felt slightly too long, especially when they sorta led to a semi-cliffhanger that wasn't especially intriguing for me. Besides that though, the last couple of logs actually laid out solid breadcrumbs towards the reveal, something that's often neglected in articles like this.

While the after action reports did fall into the overused 'Foundation leadership bad' trope to some extent, I felt enough of an emotional connection to Xi-2 throughout the exchange that I'm still willing to upvote, especially with the resistance from the agent. Good work.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was exactly my intention. Hey, maybe I'll write a compendium of these definitions one day. Might make an interesting writing exercise!

Thanks so much for the read :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love when articles hint at the Foundation's scientific definitions of basic anomalous concepts (in this case it was locked scenarios). I could read those volumes for days.

That being said, I loved this article, especially the continuation of Higashi from 4382.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad the concept of Locked Scenarios landed! I'd like to see more concepts like Reality Anchors and Humes being used for common SCP tropes. Thanks for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh no I love this. You set up an interesting premise with Locked Scenarios that elegantly takes in other phenomena from other articles, you establish a slightly quirky interesting tone between prose and clinical writing, and then on top of that you tell an excellent, uncomfortable and sad story.

Heck yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much! Spoiler text though, please :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoiler
- Title for hiding text

[When I realised the child was the source of all that I had ro give you a +1]

item.title: SCP-5858
item.content:encoded:
“I don't believe in "original sin." I don't believe in "guilt." I don't believe in villains or heroes - only right or wrong ways that individuals have taken, not by choice but by necessity or by certain still-uncomprehended influences in themselves, their circumstances, and their antecedents."

This is so simple I'm ashamed to say it, but I'm sure it's true. In fact, I would bet my life on it! And that's why I don't understand why our propaganda machines are always trying to teach us, to persuade us, to hate and fear other people on the same little world that we live in.”

― Tennessee Williams

I wasn't done playing with the concepts brought up by SCP-4382, so I fleshed them out instead. I want to see Xi-5 evolve into something more concrete, and lock down what a Locked Scenario entails. The Foundation, to me, would have classifications for something that happens so often in it's investigations of the anomalous. This was based on something I experienced as a young man. I'd like to keep working with Xi-5, and the concepts within this piece, so I hope you enjoy. As always, thank you for reading.

Image source here.

Thank you to:
Cyvstvi
MalyceGraves
Cerastes

► More T Rutherford Articles and Tales ◄
